abstract: The efficient activation and hydrogenation of CO2 into methanol represent a critical pathway for the utilization of captured carbon emissions. In this study, we explore the catalytic performance of size-selected Cu4 clusters supported on amorphous Al2O3 thin films. By employing in situ grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we monitored the oxidation state of these subnanometer clusters under near-atmospheric reaction conditions. Our results demonstrate that Cu4 clusters are exceptionally active for CH3OH synthesis, outperforming previously reported low-pressure catalysts even at low CO2 partial pressures. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations complement these findings, revealing that the unique coordination environment of the Cu4 cluster significantly lowers the activation barriers for the CO2-to-methanol pathway compared to bulk copper surfaces or larger nanoparticles. These findings suggest that precision-engineered, small copper clusters offer a highly efficient and promising route for the sustainable recycling of CO2 into value-added chemicals.
